title: Think: Does Playwright _electron API work with MSIX-packaged Ele...
question: Does Playwright _electron API work with MSIX-packaged Electron apps on Windows 11? Can Playwright attach to an already-running MSIX Electron process via CDP? What are the MSIX sandboxing constraints for remote debugging ports? Compare connectOverCDP vs _electron.launch for this scenario.

exchanges.prompt: Does Playwright _electron API work with MSIX-packaged Electron apps on Windows 11? Can Playwright attach to an already-running MSIX Electron process via CDP? What are the MSIX sandboxing constraints for remote debugging ports? Compare connectOverCDP vs _electron.launch for this scenario.
exchanges.response:
Short version: it depends almost entirely on whether your MSIX is a full-trust package (which is what electron-builder's appx target produces) or a partial-tru…
Synthesized MSIX packaging constraints against Playwright debugging capabilities
Short version: it depends almost entirely on whether your MSIX is a full-trust package (which is what electron-builder's appx target produces) or a partial-trust / AppContainerpackage. For the normal Electron MSIX case it's full-trust, and that changes everything about the sandboxing answer.
The MSIX trust distinction (this is the crux)
Electron apps packaged as MSIX declare <rescap:Capability Name="runFullTrust" />, and electron-builder adds it automatically — the runFullTrust capability is obligatory for electron apps and will be auto added if not specified. Classic Win32 apps converted to MSIX declare this special capability; the name refers to the trust level granted within the container and is not related to UAC elevation.
electron-builder
Advanced Installer
What that buys you: these run as Desktop Bridge / packagedClassicApp processes at medium integrity, not inside an AppContainer. As one breakdown puts it, for a packaged Win32 app the Integrity Level is Medium, AppContainer is No, Has Identity is Yes — though it remains subject to filesystem and registry virtualization. The uap10:TrustLevel="mediumIL" setting is what determines this versus appContainer.
Nick's .NET Travels
This matters because the famous MSIX loopback restriction is an AppContainer restriction, not a full-trust one.
Remote-debugging-port / loopback constraints
For a full-trust Electron MSIX: loopback (127.0.0.1) works normally, so a CDP server bound to localhost is reachable with no exemption. You do not need CheckNetIsolation LoopbackExempt.
For a partial-trust / AppContainer MSIX (only if you deliberately configured it that way), two things bite:
• Client loopback requires an exemption: loopback is permitted only for development purposes, and a Windows Runtime app installed outside of Visual Studio is not permitted to use it without CheckNetIsolation LoopbackExempt -a -n=<PackageFamily>.
microsoft
• More fatally for CDP, an AppContainer app can use an IP loopback only as the target address for a client network request; an app that listens on a loopback address is prevented from receiving any incoming packets. The CDP/remote-debugging endpoint isa loopback listener, so an AppContainer Electron app effectively cannot be debugged over CDP at all without a VS-style dev exemption.
microsoft
So: full-trust MSIX → no real network-isolation problem. AppContainer MSIX → CDP is essentially blocked.
Can you attach to an already-running MSIX process via CDP?
Yes in principle, but the real obstacle isn't the network — it's getting the debug flag into the process. You don't launch an MSIX app by invoking its exe with args; it's shell-activated through package identity. Practical paths:
1. Bake it in (most reliable): app.commandLine.appendSwitch('remote-debugging-port', '9222') in the main process at build time. This survives MSIX activation cleanly. Then activate the app however you like (explorer.exe shell:AppsFolder\<PackageFamilyName>!App) and attach.
2. Pass --remote-debugging-port at launch: unreliable through MSIX activation, since the package alias won't forward arbitrary flags, and there's a current Electron-level hazard regardless of packaging (see below).
Then attach the standard way — query http://localhost:9222/json/version, pull webSocketDebuggerUrl, and pass that to connectOverCDP. There's a known gotcha where passing the bare HTTP URL fails because Playwright requests /json/version/ with a trailing slash that Chrome doesn't answer cleanly; using the WebSocket URL directly is more reliable than the HTTP URL.
GitHub
connectOverCDP vs _electron.launch
These aren't interchangeable for MSIX; they target different goals.
_electron.launch launches the binary itself. Mechanically, it launches the Electron executable with --remote-debugging-port=0, then reads the allocated port from the application's stderr to establish a CDP connection, and it also attaches --inspect=0 to the Node main process. That second connection is what gives you electronApp.evaluate(({ app }) => …) against the main process — the privileged capability that makes the _electron API valuable.
DeepWiki
For MSIX this is the wrong tool in most cases:
• The binary lives under C:\Program Files\WindowsApps\<PackageFullName>, which is ACL-locked to TrustedInstaller; you generally can't point executablePath at it without permission surgery.
• Even if you launch the raw exe, you launch it without package identity — no VFS/registry redirection, no package context — so the app may not resolve its virtualized resources and won't behave like the installed MSIX app. You'd be testing the unpackaged binary, defeating the purpose.
• There's an active Electron-30+ bug independent of MSIX: Electron 30+ rejects --remote-debugging-port=0 as a CLI argument before any JavaScript executes, so it only works via app.commandLine.appendSwitch(), not as a CLI argument. Since _electron.launchpasses it on the command line, this can break launch outright on recent Electron.
GitHub
connectOverCDP attaches to an already-running, properly-activated MSIX process. Advantages: the app runs with full package identity and VFS intact (you're testing the real installed artifact), and you sidestep the WindowsApps ACL problem entirely. The cost: connectOverCDP returns a Browser, not an ElectronApplication. You get the renderer/BrowserWindow pages via browser.contexts()[0].pages(), but you lose the Electron main-process evaluate API — there's no main-process Node handle through the Chromium CDP target.
Recommendation for your scenario
To test the actual MSIX-installed app, use connectOverCDP, with the debug switch compiled into the main process (appCommandLine.appendSwitch) so MSIX activation preserves it. Activate via shell:AppsFolder\<PFN>!App, then connect to the webSocketDebuggerUrl. Accept that you won't have main-process evaluate; if you need privileged main-process assertions, expose them yourself via a test-only IPC channel or a --inspect Node-inspector connection separate from Playwright.
Reserve _electron.launch for testing the unpackaged build in CI (pointing at dist/.../YourApp.exe or main.js), where you legitimately want main-process access and none of the WindowsApps/identity constraints apply. Don't try to make it drive the installed MSIX.
One thing worth verifying on your end before committing: confirm your package's actual TrustLevel in the generated AppxManifest.xml (mediumIL vs appContainer). The entire loopback analysis above flips on that one attribute, and electron-builder's default is full-trust — but it's worth a 10-second check rather than an assumption.
